clean_story: Liquids in motion.  Gases flowing unseen. "Fluid dynamics" describes how liquids and gases move—like water swirling down a drain or wind rushing past a flag. It’s the hidden math behind why kites fly, why rivers carve canyons, and why smoke curls upward before dissolving. Imagine stirring honey versus stirring air:—Honey resists, clinging like a thick blanket. —Air slips away, invisible but pushing back when you run. This difference is viscosity—a fluid’s internal stickiness. Water has low viscosity; syrup has high. One rule governs it all:Faster flow means lower pressure. That’s why airplane wings lift—air speeds up over the curved top, pulling the wing upward. Dolphins use tiny bumps on their skin to trap turbulent water, swimming faster with less effort. Engineers copy this for ships and wind turbines. Three related subjects are chaos theory, aerodynamics, and turbulent flow.
